title: Birthright citizenship
framing: The Supreme Court ruled against a Trump administration order and in favor of universal birthright citizenship.

LF.text: Birthright citizenship is good and logical

LC.text: Universal birthright citizenship is part of what makes America great—its instant human dignity immediately granted by the state.

LD.text: American citizenship should be attainable for all people

RF.text: Citizenship is a very important thing and we should take careful consideration of how it’s granted

RC.text: Universal birthright citizenship is a wide open door with a huge incentive for migrants to break the law

RD.text: Many people with American citizenship probably shouldn’t have ever been granted citizenship in the first place, especially through birthright.

fluid.text: Birthright citizenship is good and humane policy

coalition.text: Birthright citizenship is part of what makes America such a great nation

denominated.text: Immigration is a human right, citizenship for all no matter where their parents came from

fluid.text: The government shouldn’t be offering a lifetime of entitlements to the children of immigrants

coalition.text: Immigrants are taking advantage of us

denominated.text: The government should only be granting citizenship to legacy Americans

fluid.text: The government shouldn’t be picking and choosing which newborns receive citizenship—seems like a slippery slope

coalition.text: The American empire has wreaked havoc. The children of refugees should be granted citizenship

denominated.text: Repealing the 14th amendment is white supremacy

fluid.text: It’s a loophole that’s being exploited

coalition.text: Birthright citizenship may be a good thing but there’s a loophole that’s being exploited

denominated.text: America is being invaded and we are being replaced

s-119-5.reason: Mandates federal detention of unauthorized immigrants merely *accused* (not convicted) of theft-adjacent offenses. Enacted with meaningful Democratic crossover. Splits the inclusive-citizenship coalition: institutional Democrats accepted an enforcement-forward bill to avoid "soft on crime" exposure; populist-left and civil-liberties Democrats saw due-process capitulation. This is the rare genuine same-side split on the -X (inclusive) flank.

s-118-4361.reason: The bipartisan Senate deal (Lankford-Murphy-Sinema) that collapsed under cross-pressure from both flanks. Populist right rejected it as insufficiently restrictive despite institutional GOP support; populist left rejected its asylum-processing restrictions despite institutional Democratic support. Rare four-quadrant activation — flagging explicitly.

hr-118-6062.reason: determination** — Restores local democratic control over territorial constitutional amendment, previously routed through executive-order delegation. This is the closest doctrinal cousin to birthright citizenship in the corpus: American Samoa is the one place where the citizenship clause doesn't apply of its own force, and the Insular Cases plenary-power doctrine is exactly the "who decides" question SCOTUS just revisited. Forces a choice between local self-determination (Republic-coded) and retained congressional/executive plenary control (Empire-coded).

hjres-119-1.reason: Directly pressures institutional legitimacy of the body that just ruled on birthright citizenship. Forces members to choose between locking the Court's structure via the Republic-coded amendment process versus preserving political flexibility to alter it — a proxy fight over whether the Court's authority should be insulated from majoritarian pressure.

sjres-118-68.reason: Institutional accountability mechanism (impeachment) deployed against the DHS Secretary specifically over border enforcement failures. Forces populist right's enthusiasm for impeachment-as-instrument against institutional-right discomfort with impeachment absent a clear high-crime standard, while splitting the left between defending due process seriousness and dismissing the whole proceeding as theater.

hr-118-3591.reason: Tightens asylum procedural standards through ordinary legislative process. Cross-aisle institutional bill: institutional right frames it as closing abuse loopholes through law; institutional left frames the same mechanism as due-process erosion dressed in procedural language.

sjres-119-99.reason: A Congressional Review Act resolution nullifying an agency rule that had automatically extended work permits pending adjudication. Tests whether the institutional right stands behind agency-level administrative relief (a Republic-adjacent institutional processing mechanism) or reflexively distrusts it as bureaucratic overreach — splitting business-aligned Republicans from enforcement-populist Republicans.

hr-119-2641.reason: Verify mandate for federal contractors** — Extends mandatory E-Verify to all federal contractors. Splits the right along the Y-axis: business-aligned Republicans resist added compliance burden and labor-supply friction; enforcement-populist Republicans embrace it as belonging-verification made concrete at the point of employment.

hr-118-8146.reason: Authorizes/expands state and local law enforcement's role in federal immigration enforcement — a direct federalism question about who holds enforcement authority. Splits the right: those wary of blurring federal/state enforcement lines (institutional federalism concern) versus those who see local deputization as restoring accountable, non-imperial enforcement — ironically inverting the Republic/Empire framing depending on which side of federalism you emphasize.

hr-119-30.reason: Stated purpose is protecting women; operative mechanism is a status-based sentencing/removal enhancement applying specifically to noncitizens. The framing performs protection while the mechanism performs categorical status discrimination — classic gap. Tests whether institutional Democrats reject the bill on the merits or get denominated into "soft on crime" silence.

s-118-3187.reason: Requires CBP reporting/metrics on border operations — an institutional check mechanism. Tests whether enforcement-populist members accept oversight of their own enforcement priorities as legitimate (institutional consistency) or resist transparency requirements as bureaucratic drag on Empire-style enforcement latitude.

s-119-2.reason: Broad, enacted immigration-enforcement package passed largely along expected coalitional lines. Useful as a resting-position marker: confirms where each member sits on enforcement absent cross-pressure, so departures on the higher-displacement bills above register as real movement.

billReadings.reason: Mandates federal detention of unauthorized immigrants merely *accused* (not convicted) of theft-adjacent offenses. Enacted with meaningful Democratic crossover. Splits the inclusive-citizenship coalition: institutional Democrats accepted an enforcement-forward bill to avoid "soft on crime" exposure; populist-left and civil-liberties Democrats saw due-process capitulation. This is the rare genuine same-side split on the -X (inclusive) flank.

The bipartisan Senate deal (Lankford-Murphy-Sinema) that collapsed under cross-pressure from both flanks. Populist right rejected it as insufficiently restrictive despite institutional GOP support; populist left rejected its asylum-processing restrictions despite institutional Democratic support. Rare four-quadrant activation — flagging explicitly.

billReadings.billName: To restore the ability of the people of American Samoa to approve amendments to the territorial constitution based on majority rule in a democratic act of self-determination, as authorized pursuant to an Act of Congress delegating administration of Federal territorial law in the territory to the President, and to the Secretary of the Interior under Executive Order 10264, dated June 29, 1951, under which the Constitution of American Samoa was approved and may be amended without requirement for further congressional action, subject to the authority of Congress under the Territorial Clause in article IV, section 3, clause 2 of the United States Constitution.
billReadings.reason: determination** — Restores local democratic control over territorial constitutional amendment, previously routed through executive-order delegation. This is the closest doctrinal cousin to birthright citizenship in the corpus: American Samoa is the one place where the citizenship clause doesn't apply of its own force, and the Insular Cases plenary-power doctrine is exactly the "who decides" question SCOTUS just revisited. Forces a choice between local self-determination (Republic-coded) and retained congressional/executive plenary control (Empire-coded).

billReadings.billName: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to require that the Supreme Court of the United States be composed of nine justices.
billReadings.reason: Directly pressures institutional legitimacy of the body that just ruled on birthright citizenship. Forces members to choose between locking the Court's structure via the Republic-coded amendment process versus preserving political flexibility to alter it — a proxy fight over whether the Court's authority should be insulated from majoritarian pressure.

billReadings.billName: A joint resolution providing for the issuance of a summons, providing for the appointment of a committee to receive and to report evidence, and establishing related procedures concerning the articles of impeachment against Alejandro Nicholas Mayorkas.
billReadings.reason: Institutional accountability mechanism (impeachment) deployed against the DHS Secretary specifically over border enforcement failures. Forces populist right's enthusiasm for impeachment-as-instrument against institutional-right discomfort with impeachment absent a clear high-crime standard, while splitting the left between defending due process seriousness and dismissing the whole proceeding as theater.

Tightens asylum procedural standards through ordinary legislative process. Cross-aisle institutional bill: institutional right frames it as closing abuse loopholes through law; institutional left frames the same mechanism as due-process erosion dressed in procedural language.

billReadings.billName: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services relating to "Removal of the Automatic Extension of Employment Authorization Documents".
billReadings.reason: A Congressional Review Act resolution nullifying an agency rule that had automatically extended work permits pending adjudication. Tests whether the institutional right stands behind agency-level administrative relief (a Republic-adjacent institutional processing mechanism) or reflexively distrusts it as bureaucratic overreach — splitting business-aligned Republicans from enforcement-populist Republicans.

billReadings.billName: To amend the Illegal Immigration Reform and Immigrant Responsibility Act of 1996 to require all Federal contractors to participate in the E-verify program.
billReadings.reason: Verify mandate for federal contractors** — Extends mandatory E-Verify to all federal contractors. Splits the right along the Y-axis: business-aligned Republicans resist added compliance burden and labor-supply friction; enforcement-populist Republicans embrace it as belonging-verification made concrete at the point of employment.

Authorizes/expands state and local law enforcement's role in federal immigration enforcement — a direct federalism question about who holds enforcement authority. Splits the right: those wary of blurring federal/state enforcement lines (institutional federalism concern) versus those who see local deputization as restoring accountable, non-imperial enforcement — ironically inverting the Republic/Empire framing depending on which side of federalism you emphasize.

billReadings.reason: Stated purpose is protecting women; operative mechanism is a status-based sentencing/removal enhancement applying specifically to noncitizens. The framing performs protection while the mechanism performs categorical status discrimination — classic gap. Tests whether institutional Democrats reject the bill on the merits or get denominated into "soft on crime" silence.

Requires CBP reporting/metrics on border operations — an institutional check mechanism. Tests whether enforcement-populist members accept oversight of their own enforcement priorities as legitimate (institutional consistency) or resist transparency requirements as bureaucratic drag on Empire-style enforcement latitude.

Broad, enacted immigration-enforcement package passed largely along expected coalitional lines. Useful as a resting-position marker: confirms where each member sits on enforcement absent cross-pressure, so departures on the higher-displacement bills above register as real movement.
